title: Incorrectly Calculating the Height of a Binary Tree

statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: Input to function solve(Node root) will be a tree root. Root will be a null pointer (for empty tree) or a Node object with value, left and right child pointers.
statement.outputFormat: Output to function solve(Node root) should be the maximum sum of a path in a binary tree.
statement.notes: Your solution must not use iterative methods to traverse the tree, and should handle the case where the tree is empty (root is null).

hints.text: Remember to use depth-first traversal techniques in your solution.

editorial.title: Editorial title
editorial.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: int solve(Node* root) { // TODO: implement } }
starterCode.java: public class Solution { public int solve(Node root) { // TODO: implement return 0; } }
starterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, root: 'Node') -> int: # TODO: implement return 0

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: int solve(Node* root) { if (root) return 1 + std::max(solve(root->left), solve(root->right)); else return 0; } }
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public int solve(Node root) { if (root) return 1 + Math.max(solve(root.left), solve(root.right)); else return 0; } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, root: 'Node') -> int: if root: return 1 + max(self.solve(root.left), self.solve(root.right)); else: return 0
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function solve(root) { if (root) return 1 + Math.max(solve(root.left), solve(root.right)); else return 0; }
